description: Chunking strategy catalog for RAG Studio. Defines how documents are split into chunks before embedding.

strategies.name: Fixed Size
strategies.description: Splits text into chunks of exactly N tokens, with optional overlap. The simplest and most predictable chunking method.
bestFor: When consistency matters more than semantic coherence
pros: Predictable, consistent chunk sizes
Very fast — no semantic analysis needed
Easy to reason about memory and cost
Works well with most embedding models
cons: Splits sentences and paragraphs mid-thought
May break key context across chunk boundaries
Not aware of document structure
Requires careful overlap tuning

strategies.name: Recursive Character
strategies.description: Recursively splits by a list of separators (paragraph, newline, sentence, word) until chunks are small enough. Falls back to smaller separators intelligently.
bestFor: When you want sensible splits without semantic analysis
Default choice for most RAG pipelines
pros: Respects natural text boundaries (paragraphs, sentences)
Handles most document types gracefully
Fast, no ML required
LangChain's default — well-tested
cons: Not semantically aware — may split related ideas
Custom separator lists needed for specialized formats
Can produce variable-size chunks
May not handle complex nested structures

strategies.name: Semantic Chunking
strategies.description: Uses embedding similarity to detect semantic breakpoints. Splits at positions where adjacent sentence embeddings are most dissimilar — keeping semantically related content together.
pros: Chunks contain semantically coherent ideas
Dramatically improves retrieval precision for complex queries
Works well for dense documents with topic shifts
Adapts chunk boundaries to actual content
cons: Requires an embedding model during chunking — adds cost
Slower than rule-based chunking
Variable chunk sizes (may need post-processing)
Sensitive to embedding model quality

strategies.name: Markdown Header
strategies.description: Splits markdown documents at header boundaries (H1, H2, H3). Each chunk contains a complete section and inherits header metadata for filtering.
pros: Preserves document structure and section hierarchy
Header context included as chunk metadata — enables section filtering
Natural, human-readable chunk boundaries
Excellent for technical docs with clear sections
cons: Only works for Markdown-formatted documents
Sections can be very long (large chunks)
Requires consistent markdown header usage
Sub-sections may lose parent context

strategies.name: Sentence-Based
strategies.description: Splits text at sentence boundaries using NLP-aware sentence detection (spaCy or NLTK). Groups N sentences per chunk with overlap.
pros: Never splits a sentence mid-way
Natural reading units — great for Q&A
Works well for conversational or narrative text
Good precision for fact-based queries
cons: May miss context that spans multiple paragraphs
Requires NLP library (spaCy/NLTK) — extra dependency
Slow for very large documents
Short sentences can produce tiny chunks

strategies.name: Paragraph-Based
strategies.description: Splits at paragraph boundaries (double newlines). Each paragraph becomes one or more chunks depending on size. Preserves the author's intended thought groupings.
pros: Preserves author's intended thought groupings
Chunks are naturally coherent
Simple to implement, no ML needed
Works well for most prose documents
cons: Paragraph sizes vary widely — inconsistent chunks
Very short or very long paragraphs create edge cases
Does not handle documents without paragraph breaks
Less precise for highly dense or academic text

strategies.name: Code-Aware
strategies.description: Language-aware chunking that uses AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) parsing to split at natural code boundaries: functions, classes, methods. Keeps related code together.
pros: Never splits a function or class mid-definition
Preserves logical code units (functions, classes)
Enables precise code-level retrieval
Supports language-specific separators (Python, JS, TS, etc.)
cons: Requires language detection and AST parsing
Complex implementation — different logic per language
May produce very large chunks for long classes
Not suitable for non-code documents

strategies.name: Token-Aware
strategies.description: Splits using the same tokenizer the embedding model uses (or a compatible tiktoken profile) so chunks respect real token budgets instead of raw character counts.
bestFor: Multilingual corpora where character counts mislead
pros: Chunk sizes align with provider token limits
More predictable embedding batch sizes
Fewer accidental over-length failures
cons: Requires tokenizer metadata or provider SDK
Slightly higher CPU than pure character splitters
Export runtimes must supply a matching tokenizer
